tasks.title: Temperature Conversion with Range Check
tasks.description:
Temperature conversion is a fundamental task in scientific and everyday applications. Converting between Celsius and Fahrenheit requires both arithmetic and decision-making to determine which direction the conversion should go.

Your program reads a single floating-point number representing a temperature and a character indicating the source scale ('C' for Celsius, 'F' for Fahrenheit). Convert the temperature to the other scale using the formula F = C × 9/5 + 32 (or rearranged for the reverse). Print the result rounded to one decimal place. If the converted temperature is below absolute zero (−273.15°C or −459.67°F), print an error message instead.

tasks.title: Digit Sum Calculator
tasks.description:
Summing digits is a common operation in number theory and data validation. It involves repeatedly extracting individual digits from a number and adding them together, which exercises loop control and modular arithmetic.

Your program reads a single non-negative integer. Calculate and print the sum of all its digits. For example, the digits of 4829 are 4, 8, 2, and 9, which sum to 23.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 4829 → digit sum: 4 → 12 → 14 → 23. Output: 23

tasks.title: Even/Odd Pattern Counter
tasks.description:
Detecting patterns in sequences is useful for analyzing data and understanding properties of number sequences. A simple pattern to identify is whether consecutive integers alternate between even and odd, or remain in the same parity group.

Your program reads a sequence of integers (one per line) until EOF. Count how many of them are even and how many are odd. Print both counts on separate lines. An even number is divisible by 2; an odd number is not.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 5 3 2 4 → even count: 0 → 0 → 1 → 2. Output: 2, 3

tasks.title: Multiplication Table with Conditional Formatting
tasks.description:
Multiplication tables are often printed with specific formatting rules to make them easier to read. Applying different formatting based on the values computed helps develop skills in combining arithmetic operations with conditional logic.

Your program reads two positive integers: a size n and a threshold t. Print an n × n multiplication table where entry (i, j) is the product i × j. However, if a product is less than the threshold t, print the product in square brackets; otherwise, print it without brackets. Use tab characters to separate columns.

tasks.title: Running Average Tracker
tasks.description:
Calculating a running average—the average of all values seen so far—is used in monitoring systems, quality control, and data analysis. This requires accumulating both a sum and a count, and dividing to produce an updated average after each new value.

Your program reads floating-point numbers (one per line) until EOF. After reading each number, print the running average (average of all numbers read so far) to two decimal places. Do not print anything until the first number has been read.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 10 20 30 → average: 10.00 → 15.00 → 20.00. Output: 10.00, 15.00, 20.00

tasks.title: Absolute Difference Sequence
tasks.description:
Analyzing differences between consecutive values in a sequence is useful for detecting changes and trends in data. Computing the absolute difference ensures all values are treated with the same magnitude, regardless of direction of change.

Your program reads a sequence of integers (one per line) until EOF. For each pair of consecutive integers, compute and print the absolute difference. Print one result per line.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 10 3 8 5 → differences: |10−3| → |3−8| → |8−5|. Output: 7, 5, 3

tasks.title: Grade Letter Assignment with Range Boundaries
tasks.description:
Converting numerical scores to letter grades is a common task in educational and assessment systems. This requires understanding inclusive and exclusive range boundaries and using nested or chained conditionals to assign appropriate categories.

Your program reads a single integer score between 0 and 100. Assign and print a letter grade according to the following scale: A (90–100), B (80–89), C (70–79), D (60–69), F (below 60). If the score is outside the range 0–100, print an error message.

tasks.title: Least Common Multiple Finder
tasks.description:
The least common multiple (LCM) of two integers is the smallest positive integer divisible by both. Computing the LCM is useful in scheduling, synchronization, and fraction operations. A common approach is to use the relationship: LCM(a, b) = (a × b) / GCD(a, b), where GCD is the greatest common divisor.

Your program reads two positive integers. Compute and print their least common multiple. You may compute the GCD using the Euclidean algorithm: repeatedly replace the pair (a, b) with (b, a mod b) until b becomes 0; then a is the GCD.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 12 18 → GCD steps: (12, 18) → (18, 12) → (12, 6) → (6, 0) → GCD = 6 → LCM = 12 × 18 / 6 = 36. Output: 36

tasks.title: Coin Change Counter
tasks.description:
Coin change is a practical problem used in cash registers and vending machines. Given a monetary amount, the goal is to determine how many coins of each type are needed when using the greedy algorithm—always take as many of the largest denomination as possible before moving to smaller ones.

Your program reads a single integer representing an amount in cents. Using coins of denominations 25¢ (quarter), 10¢ (dime), 5¢ (nickel), and 1¢ (penny), determine the minimum number of coins needed and print the count of each denomination in order: quarters, dimes, nickels, pennies (each on its own line).

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 41 → quarters: 1, remainder 16 → dimes: 1, remainder 6 → nickels: 1, remainder 1 → pennies: 1. Output: 1, 1, 1, 1

tasks.title: Power and Remainder Analyzer
tasks.description:
Computing powers and remainders is fundamental in cryptography, number theory, and modular arithmetic. These operations help verify divisibility properties and analyze patterns in sequences of numbers raised to powers.

Your program reads three positive integers: a base, an exponent, and a modulus. Compute base^exponent (base raised to the power of exponent), then print both the full result and the remainder when that result is divided by the modulus. The exponent may be large, so the intermediate result may overflow standard integer types—compute and print the remainder separately using modular arithmetic.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 2 10 7 → 2^10 = 1024, 1024 mod 7 = 2. Output: 1024, 2
